title: Feature Request: Input Groups
rationale: Facts: Reporter sawelch-NIVA requests Bootstrap 5 input field/button combo groups in bslib, linking directly to the Bootstrap 5 docs page showing the pattern. No environment details or version info provided. Classification hypothesis: Feature request — bslib does not currently expose Bootstrap input groups as a first-class component. Duplicate check: #1320 (Feat + Toolbars: Toolbar button groups) is scoped to the toolbar component and concerns grouping toolbar action buttons, not combining form inputs with append/prepend buttons; #298 (New custom form helpers) is a broader 2021 request and is closed; no exact duplicate found. Wrong-location check: bslib is the correct home — it is the primary package exposing Bootstrap 5 components to R/Shiny developers. Regression check: N/A — no prior support for input groups exists; this is a new capability ask. Reproduction plan: N/A for a feature request; reference is the Bootstrap 5 input-group docs. Impact: Bootstrap input groups are a common UI pattern (e.g., a text field with a prepended label or appended submit button); workaround is to write raw HTML via htmltools. Moderate interest likely among bslib users building form-heavy apps. Priority: Low — no current functionality is broken and a workaround via raw HTML exists; this is a nice-to-have enhancement. Recommended next action: Queue for team backlog; could be bundled with other Bootstrap 5 form-component additions.

title: Bookmark restore drops render-hook environments: tool-result displays with live bslib/htmltools tags fail to re-render
rationale: Facts: Reporter gadenbuie (Garrick Aden-Buie, likely a core contributor) provides a detailed root-cause analysis and a minimal reproducible example that does not require a Shiny app or LLM. The bug: jsonlite::serializeJSON/unserializeJSON round-trips bslib render-hook closures by deparsing them to source text, stripping their original environments. On restore, the reconstructed hook function lands in R_GlobalEnv and cannot resolve unexported bslib internals (e.g., tag_require_client_side), causing an uncaught error when htmltools::findDependencies() runs. The affected code path is shinychat client_get_state()/client_set_state(). The error completely breaks the restored session. Classification hypothesis: Bug — bookmark restore is a documented and supported shinychat feature, and this failure mode is a direct consequence of shinychat's serialization strategy. Duplicate check: #188 (Bookmark serialization fails when tool result contains HTMLDependency) is closed and is the Python-side analog (Pydantic serialization of HTMLDependency objects); the root cause and language differ — #188 was a Pydantic field serializer issue in Python, while #261 is an R jsonlite closure-environment issue. #190 (HTMLDependency objects not restored on bookmark restore) is also closed and Python-side. #192 (HTMLDependency objects lost during streaming) is a separate streaming issue. No open duplicate. Wrong-location check: The fix belongs in shinychat's client_get_state()/client_set_state() (or the serialization step); bslib is doing the right thing with render hooks. Regression check: Not a classic regression — this appears to be a pre-existing limitation of the jsonlite serialization approach that surfaces whenever a bslib web component (e.g., input_code_editor()) appears in a tool result display. Confirmed reproducible on shinychat 0.4.0.9000 + current bslib. Reproduction plan: Run the 3-line R MRE provided by the reporter (no app, no LLM needed): create a ContentToolResult with a bslib::input_code_editor() display, serialize/deserialize via jsonlite, call htmltools::findDependencies() on the restored tag. Impact: Breaks bookmark restore for any R shinychat app that uses tool results with bslib web components (Bootstrap web-component tags with render hooks). querychat's visualization tool is a confirmed affected consumer. The error is uncaught and kills client-side rendering. Priority: High — bookmark restore is a documented use case; the bug causes complete session failure (not a degraded experience) for a defined class of apps; known production consumers are affected. Recommended next action: Fix in shinychat client_get_state() — freeze tool-result display tags before serialization (resolve render hooks to static HTML + dependency dicts) or switch to an environment-preserving serializer for the R-side bookmark store.

title: Feature request: bookmark and restore greeting in `chat_restore()`
rationale: Facts: Reporter gadenbuie (likely core contributor) describes that chat_restore() saves/restores chat turn history but not the greeting set via chat_set_greeting(). On bookmark restore with a non-empty conversation, the greeting disappears. On restore with an empty conversation, greeting_requested fires again, regenerating a dynamic (LLM-generated) greeting at extra cost and potentially producing a different result. Reporter provides a tested workaround from querychat (~25 lines of boilerplate) and proposes a cleaner API where chat_restore() accepts a greeting reactive argument. Classification hypothesis: Feature request — extending an existing API to handle a related piece of persistent state. Duplicate check: #253 (Greetings don't participate in server-side state: not bookmarked, not readable, dismissal not observable) is closed and covered four distinct gaps in greeting state; #262 is a focused follow-up specifically requesting that chat_restore() persist the greeting, which was one sub-part of #253. Since #253 is closed and #262 is a targeted feature request with its own proposal, it is not a duplicate — it is a forward-looking ask for first-class support. Wrong-location check: shinychat is the correct home. Regression check: N/A — greeting persistence was never part of chat_restore(). Reproduction plan: Create a shinychat app with a dynamic greeting via greeting_requested + chat_set_greeting(), enable bookmarking, send a message, bookmark, reload: greeting either disappears or regenerates. Impact: Affects any shinychat app combining LLM-generated greetings with bookmark/restore. Wastes an LLM call per restore, produces non-deterministic greeting content, and degrades UX. Workaround exists and is documented (querychat pattern) but is boilerplate. Priority: Medium — well-defined feature request with moderate UX and cost impact; workaround exists. Recommended next action: Add greeting state serialization to chat_restore(); the querychat workaround code already shows the required logic. Consider a greeting reactive argument mirroring the client argument.

title: Rename chat_greeting's `dismissible` to `persistent`: the current name implies a close button that doesn't exist
rationale: Facts: Reporter cpsievert (Carson Sievert, likely a maintainer) proposes renaming the dismissible parameter of chat_greeting() to persistent. Rationale: 'dismissible' implies a user-visible close affordance (consistent with Bootstrap dismissible banners/toasts), but dismissal here is entirely system-driven (triggered by INPUT_SENT, message, chunk_start events). The Python docstring already hedges on this. Proposed change: dismissible=False → persistent=True (default behavior unchanged); a deprecation shim on dismissible covers existing code. Scope spans pkg-py (_chat_types.py, function signature), pkg-r (chat.R), TypeScript internals (transport/types.ts, chat/state.ts), tests, and docs. Classification hypothesis: Enhancement — API naming improvement with a clear deprecation path and no functional change. Duplicate check: No existing issue found requesting this rename. Wrong-location check: shinychat is correct — both R and Python packages live here. Regression check: N/A — no behavior change, only a parameter rename with a deprecation shim. Reproduction plan: N/A. Impact: Low — purely a naming improvement; no current functionality is broken. Existing code using dismissible=False continues to work via the deprecation shim. Priority: Low — API clarity improvement scheduled for a future release; no urgency, no user-facing breakage. Recommended next action: Queue for next minor release; ensure deprecation warning is added to dismissible in both R and Python and update all internal TypeScript references atomically.
